roots.title: Introduction to Modeling Concepts and Causal Inference
roots.summary: The lecture introduces Data 100, focusing on modeling concepts such as Simple Linear Regression (SLR) and multiple linear regression, outlining the course structure including mathematical derivations, model building, model selection, and performance evaluation. A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system with examples like the relationship between parents' and children's heights and the fall of an object on Earth, emphasizing accuracy and real-world influences. The importance of accurate predictions in modeling is highlighted, especially in large datasets, contrasting deep learning 'black box models' with interpretable models, using spam email detection for illustration. Causal inference is discussed as crucial for understanding variable relationships, with three primary reasons for model building: explaining complex phenomena, making accurate predictions, and establishing causal relationships. The segment addresses the challenge of distinguishing causation from correlation and the trade-off between model interpretability and prediction accuracy, emphasizing the balance between mechanistic and probabilistic models, with an example of brain wave analysis predicting seizure onset.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling Concepts and Course Structure
children.summary: The lecture introduces Data 100, focusing on modeling concepts such as Simple Linear Regression (SLR) and multiple linear regression. It outlines the course structure, including mathematical derivations, model building, model selection, and performance evaluation. A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, with examples like the relationship between parents' and children's heights and the fall of an object on Earth, emphasizing the importance of accuracy and real-world influences. The significance of accurate predictions in modeling is highlighted, especially in large datasets, contrasting deep learning 'black box models' with interpretable models, using spam email detection to illustrate the need for high accuracy.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling Concepts and Course Structure
children.summary: The lecture introduces Data 100, focusing on modeling concepts such as Simple Linear Regression (SLR) and multiple linear regression. It outlines the course structure, including mathematical derivations, model building, model selection, and performance evaluation. A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, illustrated with examples like the relationship between parents' and children's heights and the fall of an object on Earth, emphasizing the importance of accuracy and the influence of real-world factors. The significance of accurate predictions in modeling is highlighted, particularly in large datasets, contrasting deep learning 'black box models' with interpretable models, using spam email detection as an example to stress the need for high accuracy.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling Concepts and Course Structure
children.summary: The lecture welcomes students to Data 100, addressing a technical Zoom disconnection. It outlines the focus on modeling concepts, starting with Simple Linear Regression (SLR) and progressing to multiple linear regression. Upcoming lectures will cover mathematical derivations, model building, and the modeling process, including model selection and performance evaluation. A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, with an example illustrating how real-world factors can affect accuracy. The course will emphasize creating models that summarize data trends while acknowledging inherent noise.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Course Structure
children.summary: The lecture begins with a welcome to Data 100 and addresses a technical issue with Zoom that caused a disconnection. The focus of the upcoming lectures will be on the modeling phase of the course, specifically introducing modeling concepts and the Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model. The plan for the next few lectures includes an introduction to modeling, exploring different models and loss functions, and a deeper dive into multiple linear regression.

children.title: Overview of Upcoming Modeling Lectures
children.summary: The lecture will focus on mathematical derivations and model building, starting with a review of simple linear regression from Data 8. It will progress to more advanced models, culminating in a discussion of multiple regression, which involves combining multiple attributes to predict a single variable. The roadmap includes understanding the modeling process, choosing models and loss functions, fitting models, and evaluating their performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's going to be really exciting. I'm going to try to do a lot of math derivation and also building different models with you and also evaluating different, the performance of different models with you throughout today's lecture and also in the next two lectures to come. So this is where we are. We're going to start by reviewing what you've seen in in data 8, we're going to start by taking a look at what was the simple linear regression and what is modeling and what not. And we're going to go a little bit deeper into more advanced models up to the point that in the last lecture of the regression sequence, we're going to talk about multiple regression. So what happens when you have multiple different attributes that you want to model and you want to combine them into predicting one variable? Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Understanding Models in Data Science
children.summary: A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, acknowledging that it may not always be accurate due to simplifications and omitted details. For example, modeling the fall of an object under constant gravity illustrates how real-world factors like elevation and air resistance affect accuracy. In Data 100, the focus will be on creating models that summarize trends in data, such as population modeling, while recognizing that not all data points will align perfectly with the model due to inherent noise.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is a roadmap for today. We're going to start by talking about what is a model. Most of you might know what is a model or like you've used the term that there is a machine learning model and whatnot before, but just to tell you what a model is from our perspective in data 100 is that we always think about a model as an idealized representation of a system. So we say that it's an idealized representation of the system because it's not always accurate, we're not always considering all the details and all different features and all the noise that's involved in the system, but we're trying to actually have a model that kind of simulates or summarizes the trend that's happening in a system as accurately as possible. So an example of that could be modeling the fall of an object, an Earth subject to constant gravity that we always think about that as the gravity as being 9.81 meter per second square, but we all know that depending on the elevation, depending on the air resistance, this number is not accurate. So it's not going to be a constant model or a constant valley that we can use in order to model the gravity. So this is an example that we simplify assumptions, so that we have a model that's kind of roughly accurate. It's usable. It's simplifying all the dynamics of the, the dynamics in the underlying nature of the system that we have. And that's actually what we're going to do in data 100 as well. Because what you've seen in the previous lecture is that we want to model for population, but we don't have the population. We're trying to simplify assumptions, say that okay, we're sampling. Our dataset is a good sample and is a good representation of the population. And now we're going to do the modeling. And not all of our data points are going to follow the trend that we're going to to model. So those are kind of the noises that we're trying to also avoid or not directly model. Video: 1: V

leaf_chunk.audio: So this is a roadmap for today. We're going to start by talking about what is a model. Most of you might know what is a model or like you've used the term that there is a machine learning model and whatnot before, but just to tell you what a model is from our perspective in data 100 is that we always think about a model as an idealized representation of a system. So we say that it's an idealized representation of the system because it's not always accurate, we're not always considering all the details and all different features and all the noise that's involved in the system, but we're trying to actually have a model that kind of simulates or summarizes the trend that's happening in a system as accurately as possible. So an example of that could be modeling the fall of an object, an Earth subject to constant gravity that we always think about that as the gravity as being 9.81 meter per second square, but we all know that depending on the elevation, depending on the air resistance, this number is not accurate. So it's not going to be a constant model or a constant valley that we can use in order to model the gravity. So this is an example that we simplify assumptions, so that we have a model that's kind of roughly accurate. It's usable. It's simplifying all the dynamics of the, the dynamics in the underlying nature of the system that we have. And that's actually what we're going to do in data 100 as well. Because what you've seen in the previous lecture is that we want to model for population, but we don't have the population. We're trying to simplify assumptions, say that okay, we're sampling. Our dataset is a good sample and is a good representation of the population. And now we're going to do the modeling. And not all of our data points are going to follow the trend that we're going to to model. So those are kind of the noises that we're trying to also avoid or not directly model.

children.title: Understanding Models in Data Analysis
children.summary: Models are idealized representations of complex systems that, despite their inherent flaws, can provide valuable insights. In data analysis, an example is modeling the relationship between parents' and children's average heights to create a simple, interpretable model that maintains reasonable accuracy. Another example discussed is modeling the fall of an object on Earth, which assumes constant acceleration due to gravity at 9.81 m/s², while acknowledging that it does not consider factors like air resistance.

children.title: Understanding Models in Data Analysis
children.summary: Models are idealized representations of complex systems, and while all models are inherently flawed, some can still provide useful insights. For instance, in data analysis, we might model the relationship between parents' average heights and children's average heights. The goal is to create a simple, interpretable model that remains reasonably accurate despite the complexities involved. An example is modeling the fall of an object on Earth, which assumes constant acceleration due to gravity at 9.81 m/s², acknowledging that it does not account for factors like air resistance.

children.title: Importance of Accurate Predictions in Modeling
children.summary: Accurate predictions are essential in modeling, particularly with large datasets. Deep learning architectures, characterized by millions of parameters, function as 'black box models' where parameter relationships are not easily discernible. Despite their complexity, these models often outperform interpretable models in accuracy. An example provided is spam email detection, highlighting the need to classify emails correctly to prevent missing important messages or misclassifying spam, emphasizing the priority of high accuracy over model interpretability.

children.title: Importance of Accurate Predictions in Modeling
children.summary: Accurate predictions are crucial in modeling, especially when dealing with large datasets. Deep learning architectures utilize millions of parameters, making them 'black box models' where the relationships between parameters are not easily identifiable. Despite this complexity, these models often provide greater accuracy compared to interpretable models. An example discussed is spam email detection, where the goal is to classify emails correctly to avoid missing important messages or misclassifying spam. The emphasis is on achieving high accuracy, even at the expense of model interpretability.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The second reason is that We might want to have really accurate productions we have a good amount of data we want to make sure that we have very good accurate productions and in doing so what we will do is we'll throw in multiple different parameters not multiple but millions of parameters into a deep learning architecture for example in order to have a very accurate production. So what's happening when we want to use these black box models, we call them black box models because there's millions of different parameters. It's not easily, we cannot easily identify what's the relationship between them or what is the interaction between different parameters that is bits out, the actual estimation that we have at the end. But what we know is that over the population or over the sample that we have, we would say that it's accurate enough that we can actually use it. And it's more accurate than most of the interpretable models or simple models that we've seen in the first case. So examples could be if you want to have a spam email detection. So this is kind of a project that you will do in the last project of data 100. We're going to give you emails with subject line, with the text in the body, and then you will be classifying emails into class of a spam versus not a spam. What you want is you really want to be accurate here. You don't want to have a spam email landing in your inbox or vice versa, missing an important email and moving that into your spam folder. So this is very important. In these cases, we would say that the reason that we want to do the model is to have a very accurate prediction, even if it comes at the cost of the model, not being interpretable. leaf_chunk.audio: The second reason is that We might want to have really accurate productions we have a good amount of data we want to make sure that we have very good accurate productions and in doing so what we will do is we'll throw in multiple different parameters not multiple but millions of parameters into a deep learning architecture for example in order to have a very accurate production. So what's happening when we want to use these black box models, we call them black box models because there's millions of different parameters. It's not easily, we cannot easily identify what's the relationship between them or what is the interaction between different parameters that is bits out, the actual estimation that we have at the end. But what we know is that over the population or over the sample that we have, we would say that it's accurate enough that we can actually use it. And it's more accurate than most of the interpretable models or simple models that we've seen in the first case. So examples could be if you want to have a spam email detection. So this is kind of a project that you will do in the last project of data 100. We're going to give you emails with subject line, with the text in the body, and then you will be classifying emails into class of a spam versus not a spam. What you want is you really want to be accurate here. You don't want to have a spam email landing in your inbox or vice versa, missing an important email and moving that into your spam folder. So this is very important. In these cases, we would say that the reason that we want to do the model is to have a very accurate prediction, even if it comes at the cost of the model, not being interpretable.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Building Reasons
children.summary: Causal inference is crucial for understanding the relationships between variables, such as establishing whether smoking causes lung cancer rather than merely correlating with it. In model building, three primary reasons are identified: explaining complex phenomena, making accurate predictions, and establishing causal relationships. The segment highlights the challenge of distinguishing causation from correlation, noting that many statistical tools can show associations but do not confirm causation. Additionally, it discusses the trade-off between model interpretability and prediction accuracy.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Building Reasons
children.summary: Causal inference is essential for understanding relationships between variables, such as determining if smoking causes lung cancer rather than just correlating it. In model building, three key reasons are highlighted: explaining complex phenomena, making accurate predictions, and establishing causal relationships. The difficulty of distinguishing causation from correlation is emphasized, noting that many statistical tools can indicate associations but do not confirm causation. Furthermore, a trade-off exists between model interpretability and prediction accuracy.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Building Reasons
children.summary: Causal inference, though not covered in Data 100, is crucial for understanding variable relationships, such as determining if smoking causes lung cancer rather than merely correlating it. In model building, three main reasons are emphasized: explaining complex phenomena, making accurate predictions, and establishing causal relationships. The challenge of distinguishing causation from correlation is highlighted, as many statistical tools can infer associations but do not confirm causation. Additionally, there is a trade-off between model interpretability and prediction accuracy.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Building Reasons
children.summary: Causal inference is a topic not covered in Data 100 but is essential for understanding relationships between variables, such as determining if smoking causes lung cancer rather than just correlating smoking with lung cancer. In the context of model building, three main reasons are highlighted: to explain complex phenomena, to make accurate predictions, and to establish causal relationships. The discussion emphasizes the challenge of distinguishing causation from correlation, noting that while many statistical tools are designed to infer associations, they do not necessarily confirm causation. Additionally, there is a trade-off between creating interpretable models and achieving high prediction accuracy.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Reason tree is a reason that we will not go over in data 100, but that's a topic for more advanced courses, some stats courses or data 102 covers causal inference. So for example, if you want to figure out if a smoking causes cancers, not correlation between a smoking and a person having lung cancer, but figuring out the causation between causation relationship between a variable and another variable or a response output, in those cases, we do causal inference. Again, not the topic for data 100, but just so that you know that everything that we're modeling in a stage one and two, it's mostly around finding a function that maps input to an output. But what we're looking at in the recent tree is to actually have a causal inference kind of argument and figuring out if a variable not only explains the output, but also causes the output. children.title: Balancing Model Interpretability and Understanding Correlation
children.summary: The discussion emphasizes balancing interpretability and accuracy in data science models, highlighting mechanistic models based on established relationships versus probabilistic models for classification. An example of brain wave analysis illustrates how probabilistic models can predict seizure onset in anxiety monitoring. The segment also covers simple linear regression, detailing its definition, derivation of the regression line equation, and the calculations for slope and intercept. Additionally, the correlation coefficient is introduced as a measure of linear association between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1, with explanations of positive and negative correlations, zero correlation, and the calculation using Pearson's correlation coefficient. Covariance is also introduced as a related concept.

children.title: Balancing Model Interpretability and Accuracy in Data Science
children.summary: The discussion emphasizes the need to balance interpretability and accuracy in data science models, distinguishing between mechanistic models, which rely on established relationships, and probabilistic models used for classification. An example of brain wave analysis is presented, demonstrating how probabilistic models can predict seizure onset, particularly in monitoring anxiety conditions. The segment also covers simple linear regression, including its definition, derivation of the regression line equation, and the components of a regression model, specifically the calculations for slope and intercept.

children.title: Balancing Model Interpretability and Accuracy
children.summary: The discussion focuses on balancing interpretability and accuracy in data science models, highlighting the distinction between mechanistic (deterministic) models, which are based on established relationships and measurements, and probabilistic models used for classification tasks. An example involving brain wave analysis illustrates how probabilistic models can predict seizure onset based on recorded data, emphasizing their utility in monitoring conditions like anxiety. The segment also introduces simple linear regression, covering its definition, derivation of the regression line equation, and a previous example from Data 8. Additionally, it explains the components of a regression model, including the calculation of the slope and intercept.

children.title: Balancing Interpretability and Accuracy in Models
children.summary: Interpretability and accuracy are crucial aspects in data science, and the goal is to find a balance between the two. While some models may lack interpretability, the ideal scenario is to have models that are both interpretable and sufficiently accurate. There are two main types of models: mechanistic (deterministic) models, which follow a non-probabilistic approach based on measurements and established relationships between variables, and probabilistic models, which are often used for classification tasks. An example of a probabilistic model is presented, involving research on brain waves to assess anxiety levels.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, others go pernata 100. Throughout the explanation of this reason one, two, three, I tried to say that interpretability is important and also accuracy is important. So what we'll try to do generally as data scientists is to strike the balance between interpretability and accuracy. So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved. When they measure, they measure it, the values is there, and then they measure multiple different variables and they basically look at the relationship between them. There are models that are probabilistic. You will see one example of a probabilistic model towards the end of data 100, and mostly we will see probabilistic models when we want to do classification or identification of a certain task. So an example of that, I brought it here from one of my papers that is the work of my PhD student looking into studying brain waves in order to figure out the anxiety levels of subjects. leaf_chunk.audio: Again, others go pernata 100. Throughout the explanation of this reason one, two, three, I tried to say that interpretability is important and also accuracy is important. So what we'll try to do generally as data scientists is to strike the balance between interpretability and accuracy. So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved. When they measure, they measure it, the values is there, and then they measure multiple different variables and they basically look at the relationship between them. There are models that are probabilistic. You will see one example of a probabilistic model towards the end of data 100, and mostly we will see probabilistic models when we want to do classification or identification of a certain task. So an example of that, I brought it here from one of my papers that is the work of my PhD student looking into studying brain waves in order to figure out the anxiety levels of subjects.

children.title: Probabilistic Models in Brain Wave Analysis
children.summary: Brain waves are modeled as random processes using probabilistic models to understand their interactions and concentration in specific areas, which can lead to conditions like anxiety or seizures. These models help predict the likelihood of seizure onset based on recorded brain wave data over a period, enabling closer monitoring of patients if the probability is sufficiently high. The discussion also contrasts mechanistic or deterministic models, which do not involve probability, with probabilistic models that will be explored further in Data 100.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So brain waves by themselves are random processes that you're modeling. There are probabilistic models that basically you will try to study and figure out how they travel in brain and how they interact with each other and how would they concentrate in a certain area so that it causes anxiety or it causes, for example, seizure. This is kind of the modeling that they would do in order to predict whether someone is going to have an onset of seizure in future or not just looking at those probabilistic models. And then you will basically have a probabilistic model that will say that with this specific likelihood, this subject that we had to recording up their brain waves over the last, like for example, seven days is going to have seizure in, for example, in the next 10 minutes. And if that probability is high enough then basically in patient facilities, they'll try to monitor that patient more accurately or more closely. So this is the kind of different categories of models that we're looking at. Mechanistic or deterministic, there's no probability involved and probabilistic models that again you will see later on in data 100. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

leaf_chunk.audio: So brain waves by themselves are random processes that you're modeling. There are probabilistic models that basically you will try to study and figure out how they travel in brain and how they interact with each other and how would they concentrate in a certain area so that it causes anxiety or it causes, for example, seizure. This is kind of the modeling that they would do in order to predict whether someone is going to have an onset of seizure in future or not just looking at those probabilistic models. And then you will basically have a probabilistic model that will say that with this specific likelihood, this subject that we had to recording up their brain waves over the last, like for example, seven days is going to have seizure in, for example, in the next 10 minutes. And if that probability is high enough then basically in patient facilities, they'll try to monitor that patient more accurately or more closely. So this is the kind of different categories of models that we're looking at. Mechanistic or deterministic, there's no probability involved and probabilistic models that again you will see later on in data 100.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment covers the basics of simple linear regression, including the definition and derivation of the regression line equation. It references a previous example from Data 8, where the regression line was used to predict the number of characters in chapters of 'Little Women' based on punctuation counts. The discussion emphasizes the importance of understanding derivatives and optimizing objective functions, which will be defined later in the lecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we're going to do in data 100 right now as a starting point, just to get warmed up as to take a look at simple linear regression and what you'd see in data 8. So we're going to try go over regression line and the definition for regression line and actually derive the equation for regression line that you'd seen. But this time we're going to actually use a little bit of calculus in this lecture to just get warm up because you will need to get comfortable with actually doing derivatives and calculating or optimizing an objective function and whatnot. I'm going to tell you what objective function is by the end of today's lecture. So let's dive in. You've seen this regression line from before, right? So you've seen that in data aid, this was a famous plot that you had that was predicting on different chapters of little women novel that using a number of periods, period punctuations in a chapter, it was trying to predict the number of characters that would appear in that specific chapter. You would see that it tries to modulate using regression line and then we're going to take a look at the equations for that regression line and HUD data aid arrived at that regression line. children.title: Regression Model Components and Estimation
children.summary: The slope of a regression line is defined as the correlation (R) multiplied by the standard deviation of Y divided by the standard deviation of X. The intercept, or Y intercept, occurs when X equals zero and is calculated as the average of Y minus the slope times the average of X. Memorization of equations is not necessary, as understanding two key aspects of a model allows for derivation of all equations. Regression estimation involves using the model to predict Y values by plugging in X values into the equation: intercept plus slope times the value. The regression line uniquely minimizes the mean squared error among all straight lines.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You also see that in data aid, we defined a slope as correlation or R times the standard deviation of Y over the standard deviation of X. This is your Y dimension and this is your X dimension and correlation. I'm going to formula define it in a sec. An intercept, we talked about intercept or the point that when X is equal to zero, your Y value is non-zero probably, or that's called Y intercept or intercept was defined as average of Y minus slope times average of X. And we're going to see why that's the case. So you don't need to actually memorize all of these equations anymore. You just need to know two things about a model in order to drive all the equations yourself. So you're going to be released from memorizing kind of task from this point in time. We also talked about regression estimation. So regression estimation is that, okay, you have a model or estimation on any model, per se, is that we have a model and we're gonna just plug in the value that we wanna do prediction on into the equation and the equation was intercept plus slope times the value and then that would give you the regression estimation. children.title: Understanding Residuals and Regression Line
children.summary: Residuals are defined as the difference between observed values and estimated values, and they are visually represented on a plot. The goal in data modeling is to minimize these residuals across the dataset, indicating that the model should fit the data points as accurately as possible. The regression line is specifically the straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, with the equation for the regression estimate being the intercept plus the slope multiplied by the independent variable. An example is provided where the number of characters in each chapter of 'Little Women' is estimated based on the number of periods.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There is a concept of residual that we're going to play a lot with and residual is the fine as observed value minus the estimated value. Okay, so the residuals are actually shown here on the plot. This is a residual. This is a residual. This is a residual and so on and so forth. What we're trying to do in data 100 and true out the modeling is that we're looking at the residuals that we're accumulating true out the entire data set and we're trying to minimize the amount of residual and I'm going to define what amount of residual means formally using a function mathematical function later on in today's lecture, but just know that if a point is not on the line, that's an imperfect prediction and we're going to measure how imperfect it is and we're going to try to make sure that the model that we're using is going to be a real we're fitting to the data is passing through all the data points as accurately as possible. children.title: Understanding Residuals and Regression Line
children.summary: Residuals can be positive or negative depending on their position relative to the regression line, with positive residuals for points above the line and negative for those below. The regression line minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, represented by the equation: regression estimate = intercept + slope x x. This segment also discusses the relationship between positive and negative residuals, indicating that the line typically passes through the middle of the data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One observation here is that some residuals are positive, some residuals are negative. What you're seeing here is that, for example, for this specific data point, the estimation is exactly this, sorry, the actual value is this value right here on the y-axis. And the estimated value is the projection of that point onto the line, and then you would see what that value is, right? So this is my ground truth why and this is why hat. I put hat as a representation of this is the predicted value or the estimated value. And you can see that based on this equation, the residual is a positive value for the points that are above the line. And you can also rationalize in the same way that for any point that's below the line, the residual is a negative value. You will see later on that there is a relationship between all of these residuals that are positive under residuals that are negative. And that's why the line generally passes through in the middle of the data point. So you have some positive residuals and some negative residuals. And we're going to see the... Mathematics magic behind why that's the case. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

You will see later on that there is a relationship between all of these residuals that are positive under residuals that are negative. children.title: Understanding Correlation and Standardization
children.summary: Correlation is defined as the average of the product of X and Y when both are measured in a standard unit. A variable in standard unit is zero-centered with a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. To standardize data, calculate the average of the values, subtract this average from each sample to achieve zero centering, and then divide by the standard deviation to ensure a standard deviation of one. The product of X and Y, both measured in standard units, is averaged by summing the products and dividing by the number of samples.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's revisit correlation. So these are all reminders. So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense. What was correlation? So I want to break down the definition of correlation here for you. The definition from data aid textbook says that correlation is the average of the product of X and Y. When both are measured in a standard unit. Right? Three pieces of information. And let me tell you what they are. It's starting from measuring in a standard unit. When we say that a variable is an standard unit, that means that it's zero center to center deviation of one. Right? So the average of all the values should be zero. And then the standard deviation of all of the values should be equal to one. And the way that we actually standardize a data is to go through this process right here, that you take one column of values or one set of values. You calculate their average, you subtract the average from each individual sample. So that's how you will make sure that it's zero centered, meaning that the mean is at zero. And then in order to make sure that the standard deviation is equal to one, you divide all of these transformations by standard deviation of that entire column or that set of values. This ensures that the standard deviation is equal to one as well. So that's what we mean by a standardization. So that's a standard unit. So when we say product of X and Y, both measured in a standard unit, this means that X measured in a standard unit is right here. Y measured in a standard unit is right here. So that's X and Y measured in a standard unit. And then there was an additional piece that says that the average of those products, and you're seeing that here I'm taking average by summing up all of these products and then dividing it by one over n, which is the average, right? leaf_chunk.audio: So let's revisit correlation. So these are all reminders. So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense. What was correlation? So I want to break down the definition of correlation here for you. The definition from data aid textbook says that correlation is the average of the product of X and Y. When both are measured in a standard unit. Right? Three pieces of information. And let me tell you what they are. It's starting from measuring in a standard unit. When we say that a variable is an standard unit, that means that it's zero center to center deviation of one. Right? So the average of all the values should be zero. And then the standard deviation of all of the values should be equal to one. And the way that we actually standardize a data is to go through this process right here, that you take one column of values or one set of values. You calculate their average, you subtract the average from each individual sample. So that's how you will make sure that it's zero centered, meaning that the mean is at zero. And then in order to make sure that the standard deviation is equal to one, you divide all of these transformations by standard deviation of that entire column or that set of values. This ensures that the standard deviation is equal to one as well. So that's what we mean by a standardization. So that's a standard unit. So when we say product of X and Y, both measured in a standard unit, this means that X measured in a standard unit is right here. Y measured in a standard unit is right here. So that's X and Y measured in a standard unit. And then there was an additional piece that says that the average of those products, and you're seeing that here I'm taking average by summing up all of these products and then dividing it by one over n, which is the average, right?

children.title: Standardization and Pearson's Correlation Coefficient
children.summary: The data set consists of a data frame with two columns, x and y, containing n data points. Each row is processed to zero center or standardize the values. After standardization, the product of the standardized x and y values for each row is calculated. The segment also defines Pearson's correlation coefficient, r, as the average of the product of x and y in standard units, incorporating the standard deviations of x and y. Additionally, a side note mentions the concept of covariance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what is i, what is n? And let's go back to our data set. So I'm assuming that in this data set, I have a data frame with two columns. I'm going to call one column column x, one column column y, and then the rows are row one, row two, and then all the way to row n. So assume that I have n data points. So this data set d has n data points with row one b x1 y1, row two b x2 y2, and then all the way to x and yn. So what I need to do is go through each one of those rows, zero center or standardize all of those values. Once all of those values are standardized, I will basically try to calculate the product of the two x standard unit, y standard unit in each given row, and then I have a value. children.title: Calculating Correlation and Covariance
children.summary: To calculate the average of products measured by x and y, sum the values and divide by n. This correlation is known as Pearson's coefficient, which follows a consistent formula regardless of the terminology used. If data is not standardized but zero-centered, the same formula can be used to compute covariance, defined as the average of the product of the means removed. Covariance relates to correlation as it equals correlation multiplied by the standard deviations of x and y. Correlation indicates the linear association between two variables but does not imply causation or non-linear relationships. A high correlation suggests the appropriateness of applying simple linear regression, while a lack of linear relationship indicates regression modeling may not be suitable.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then all of those values need to be summed up together and then divided by, or multiplied by 1 over n to calculate the average of those products that were measured, that were x and y measured instead of unit. That's what it is. It's a complicated kind of equation, but once you remember what it was, it will make sense. This correlation is also called Pearson's coefficient or Pearson's correlation coefficient as well. So if you've heard multiple different terms for correlation, they all mean the correlation, and they all follow the same formula. If we don't want to standardize the data in a way that every column has a standard deviation of 1, But we want to make sure that there's zero centred, but they keep their standard deviation applying the similar formula is going to give us covariance. Covariance is basically meaning that remove the means, multiply them, and then pick the average. And this is the covariance. And covariance is equal to obviously based on the equation, is equal to correlation times the standard deviation of x times the standard deviation of y. These are repetitions from data aid just so that you know where that correlation was coming from when we were calculating this slope and kind of getting a sense of the fact that we needed to have correlation in order to figure out the relationship between X and X and Y. What we also know that is that the correlation shows us the linear association. It does not show us non-linear association. It does not show us causation in any sense. It just shows us the linear association or the degree of linear association between two variables x and y. And when the correlation is high enough, then in that case, it will make sense to just run simple linear regression or any regression analysis. Otherwise, if you don't see a linear relationship between two variables, why would you go and model it using a regression line? Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

leaf_chunk.audio: And then all of those values need to be summed up together and then divided by, or multiplied by 1 over n to calculate the average of those products that were measured, that were x and y measured instead of unit. That's what it is. It's a complicated kind of equation, but once you remember what it was, it will make sense. This correlation is also called Pearson's coefficient or Pearson's correlation coefficient as well. So if you've heard multiple different terms for correlation, they all mean the correlation, and they all follow the same formula. If we don't want to standardize the data in a way that every column has a standard deviation of 1, But we want to make sure that there's zero centred, but they keep their standard deviation applying the similar formula is going to give us covariance. Covariance is basically meaning that remove the means, multiply them, and then pick the average. And this is the covariance. And covariance is equal to obviously based on the equation, is equal to correlation times the standard deviation of x times the standard deviation of y. These are repetitions from data aid just so that you know where that correlation was coming from when we were calculating this slope and kind of getting a sense of the fact that we needed to have correlation in order to figure out the relationship between X and X and Y. What we also know that is that the correlation shows us the linear association. It does not show us non-linear association. It does not show us causation in any sense. It just shows us the linear association or the degree of linear association between two variables x and y. And when the correlation is high enough, then in that case, it will make sense to just run simple linear regression or any regression analysis. Otherwise, if you don't see a linear relationship between two variables, why would you go and model it using a regression line?

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Covariance
children.summary: The correlation coefficient, denoted as ?, measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1. A value close to 1 indicates a perfectly positive association, while -1 indicates a perfectly negative one. It is calculated using Pearson's correlation coefficient, which averages the product of standardized x and y values. Positive correlation means that as one variable increases, the other does as well, while negative correlation indicates the opposite. A correlation of zero signifies no linear association, and the absolute value of the correlation coefficient is always less than 1. Examples are provided to illustrate cases of no clear correlation, and covariance is introduced as a related concept.

children.title: Understanding Correlation: Definition and Implications
children.summary: The correlation coefficient, denoted as ?, measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1. A correlation close to 1 indicates a perfectly positive association, while -1 indicates a perfectly negative one. The calculation involves standardizing data points and is expressed as Pearson's correlation coefficient, which is the average of the product of x and y in standard units. Positive correlation means as one variable increases, the other does too, whereas negative correlation indicates that as one increases, the other decreases. A correlation of zero signifies no linear association. The absolute value of the correlation coefficient is always less than 1, and examples illustrate cases of no clear correlation. Covariance is also discussed as a related concept.

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Its Range
children.summary: The correlation coefficient, denoted as ?, measures the strength of a linear association between two variables and ranges from -1 to 1. A correlation close to 1 indicates a perfectly positive linear association, while a correlation of -1 indicates a perfectly negative association. The calculation of correlation involves standardizing data points and can be expressed as Pearson's correlation coefficient, which is the average of the product of x and y in standard units. Additionally, covariance is related to correlation and is calculated using the formula for the average of the product of deviations from the mean.

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Its Implications
children.summary: Positive correlation indicates that as one variable (x) increases, the other variable (y) also increases, resulting in a positive slope. Conversely, negative correlation means that as x increases, y decreases, leading to a negative slope. A correlation of zero signifies a weak linear association. The segment also defines Pearson's correlation coefficient, which is the average of the product of x and y in standard units, and explains that correlation measures the strength of the linear relationship between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What is positive? What does negative mean? Positive means that by increasing x, y is also always increasing. So that's a positive slope, right? Negative correlation means that by negative is strong correlation means that by increasing x, your y is going to decrease. So that's a negative slope and that's why correlation that you have is going to control whether you're going to get a positive slope or a negative slope in your dataset or in your linear regression. When correlation is equal to zero, it shows that we have a weak linear association. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Its Implications
children.summary: Correlation measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1. When the correlation coefficient (R) is zero, it indicates that the variables are uncorrelated, meaning changes in one do not influence the other. The absolute value of R is always less than 1, and examples illustrate cases where no clear correlation exists between the variables. Additionally, Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as the average of the product of x and y in standard units, with covariance also being discussed as a related concept.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It doesn't tell us that there is no association between them. It just says that the linear association is weak if you're concerned with linear association. And obviously I said that linear association does not mean causation. In any sense, it just means linear association for our purposes. When R is equal to zero, we would also say that the two variables are uncorrelated, meaning that there is no correlation between them. There is no reason that one increasing or decreasing one is going to have any influence or is going to show any influence on the other one. So there is no correlation between them. We would say that they're uncorrelated. correlation is a value between negative 1 and 1. So that's what I'm showing that the absolute value of r is always less than 1. And here are some examples. For example, in the first case, you're seeing that there is absolutely no clear sign that there is correlation or linear association between x and y. Right. So take the x axis always as x and then y axis as a second variable. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Correlation and Regression Concepts Explained
children.summary: The segment covers correlation and linear associations, explaining that a strong positive correlation near 1 indicates a strong linear relationship, while a negative correlation of -0.7 suggests a weaker association due to data noise. It notes that a quadratic relationship yields a correlation near zero, indicating no linear pattern. Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as a measure of the strength of the linear association between two variables, calculated as the average of the product of x and y in standard units. The slope of the regression line in standard units equals the correlation coefficient, with positive R values indicating an increasing trend and negative R values indicating a decreasing trend. The regression line is expressed as y = r * x in standard units, transforming to y = r * (SD of y / SD of x) * (x - average of x) + average of y in original units, illustrating how scaling affects the relationship. The segment also discusses the simplification and derivation of the regression equation to isolate the predicted value (y hat) and explains the manipulation of the equation to arrive at the final form.

children.title: Correlation and Regression Concepts Explained
children.summary: The segment explains correlation and linear associations, detailing that a strong positive correlation near 1 indicates a strong linear relationship, while a negative correlation of -0.7 shows a weaker association due to data noise. It highlights that a quadratic relationship results in a correlation near zero, indicating no linear pattern. Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as a measure of the strength of the linear association between two variables, calculated as the average of the product of x and y in standard units. The regression line's slope in standard units equals the correlation coefficient, with positive R values indicating an increasing trend and negative R values indicating a decreasing trend. The regression line is expressed as y = r * x in standard units, transforming to y = r * (SD of y / SD of x) * (x - average of x) + average of y in original units, demonstrating how scaling affects the relationship. The segment also covers the simplification and derivation of the regression equation to isolate the predicted value (y hat) and its components, explaining the manipulation of the equation to arrive at the final form.

children.title: Correlation and Linear Associations Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses the correlation between variables x and y, detailing different scenarios of linear associations. A significant positive correlation close to 1 indicates a strong linear relationship, while a negative correlation of -0.7 demonstrates a weaker association due to data noise. It is emphasized that a quadratic relationship results in a correlation near zero, highlighting the absence of a linear pattern. The definition of correlation is also provided, describing it as the average of the product of x and y in standard units.

children.title: Correlation and Linear Associations Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses the correlation between variables x and y, highlighting different scenarios of linear associations. It explains a significant positive correlation close to 1, indicating a strong linear relationship. In another case, a negative correlation of -0.7 is presented, showing a weaker linear association due to data noise. The discussion emphasizes that while there may be a relationship between x and y, a quadratic relationship results in a correlation near zero, as there is no linear pattern. The definition of correlation is also referenced, explaining it as the average of the product of x and y in standard units.

children.title: Correlation and Regression in Standard Units
children.summary: Pearson's correlation coefficient measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, calculated as the average of the product of x and y in standard units. When data is zero-centered, the regression line passes through the origin, and in standard units, the slope of the regression line equals the correlation coefficient (r). Correlation indicates the relationship between variables X and Y, with positive R values signifying an increasing trend and negative R values indicating a decreasing trend. The regression line in standard units is expressed as y = r * x, but in original units, it transforms to y = r * (SD of y / SD of x) * (x - average of x) + average of y. This illustrates how scaling affects the relationship between x and y.

children.title: Correlation and Regression in Standard Units
children.summary: Correlation, specifically Pearson's correlation coefficient, measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, calculated as the average of the product of x and y in standard units. When data is zero-centered, the regression line passes through the origin, and in standard units, the slope of the regression line equals the correlation coefficient (r). This relationship is essential for understanding simple linear regression.

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Regression Lines
children.summary: Correlation indicates the relationship between variables X and Y, where a positive R value signifies an increasing trend and a negative R value indicates a decreasing trend. The regression line, when expressed in standard units, passes through the origin with the equation y = r * x. However, to reflect the original data composition, the regression line should pass through the averages of X and Y instead of the origin, necessitating a transformation of the data points back to their original units.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then we talk about the correlation. That means that by every one unit increase in X, the Y value is going to increase by our unit. And again, notice that if R is positive, that means that it's an increasing trend, when R is negative, that means that that's a decreasing trend, that's a negative slope, and that's how you can make sense of what's happening in the regression kind of line. But we don't want to live in the standard unit word. What we want to live in is in the original unit or the original kind of composition of the data set that we had. So what we're going to do is we're going to move back all of our data all of our data sets or data points into the stand into the original units that they were meaning that our data point or our. A regression line is going to pass through average of x and average of y instead of passing through the origin. Right. Again, because we removed the, removed average of x from all of the x values and removed average of y from all the y values. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

But we don't want to live in the standard unit word. What we want to live in is in the original unit or the original kind of composition of the data set that we had. So what we're going to do is we're going to move back all of our data all of our data sets or data points into the stand into the original units that they were meaning that our data point or our. A regression line is going to pass through average of x and average of y instead of passing through the origin. Right. Again, because we removed the, removed average of x from all of the x values and removed average of y from all the y values.

children.title: Regression Line in Standard and Original Units
children.summary: When variables x and y are measured in standard units, the regression line for predicting y based on x has a slope of r and passes through the origin, represented by the equation y = r * x. In the original units of the data, the regression line is expressed as y = r * (SD of y / SD of x) * (x - average of x) + average of y. This demonstrates how scaling affects the relationship between x and y, with changes in y corresponding to r times the standard deviation of y for each standard deviation unit of x.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: When we added back, then it will basically push all of the data points by a factor of average of x and average of y. And that's why the origin is going to move to the point that corresponds to average of x and average of y. What you're seeing here is that x is the scale appropriately. Here we would say that for every standard deviation unit of x, y is going to be changed by r times the standard deviation of y. Right? That's how, again, you're scaling across the x, a scaling across the y because previously you divided by standard deviation of x and a standard deviation of y. If you multiply that back, this is what you're going to get. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.summary: The regression line for predicting y based on x in standard units has a slope of r and passes through the origin, represented by the equation y = r * x. In original units, the equation transforms to y - ȳ = r * (SD of y / SD of x) * (x - x̄), where ȳ and x̄ are the averages of y and x, respectively.

This is kind of moving all of these, all of the points or all of the equations from the top equation down to the bottom equation, but moving it to non-standard unit. children.title: Regression Line in Standard and Original Units
children.summary: The regression line for predicting y based on x in standard units has a slope of r and passes through the origin, represented by the equation y = r * x. When expressed in the original units of the data, the equation transforms to y - average of y = r * (x - average of x) * (SD of y / SD of x), incorporating the standard deviations of y and x.

children.title: Regression Equation Simplification and Derivation
children.summary: The segment covers the simplification of the regression equation to isolate the predicted value (y hat) and its components, specifically the slope (B hat) and intercept. It explains the manipulation of the equation by moving terms and multiplying by the standard deviation of y, resulting in the final form y hat = r * (standard deviation of y / standard deviation of x) * (x - x bar) + y bar. The derivation of the regression line equation is discussed, highlighting that the slope (b hat) equals the correlation coefficient R times the standard deviation of Y divided by the standard deviation of X, indicating the increase in Y for each standard unit increase in X. The intercept is defined as y bar minus the slope multiplied by x, allowing for direct calculation using the slope. Additionally, the regression line equation y = a + b*x is defined, with 'a' as the intercept and 'b' as the slope, and the concept of residuals is introduced as the difference between actual and predicted values.

children.title: Simplifying Regression Equation and Coefficients
children.summary: The segment discusses the simplification of the regression equation to isolate the predicted value (y hat) and its components. The speaker explains the process of moving terms around to achieve the desired form, specifically isolating the slope (B hat) and intercept. The equation is manipulated by multiplying both sides by the standard deviation of y and moving y bar to the other side. The final form of the equation is presented as y hat = r * (standard deviation of y / standard deviation of x) * (x - x bar) + y bar, emphasizing the goal of isolating coefficients related to x.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's the equation that we'll be working with. If I move this equation to this address slide and try to simplify and just make sure that why hat remains on one side and then everything else is going to be remained on the right hand side and then I'm basically trying to get the coefficient, get a hat which is your intercept and also be hat which was your slope then we can basically figure out what is the the interest of equation and what is the, what is this little equation in our case. So let me start doing that. From this point and on, I might write equations on this slide, but when I publish this slide, the slides are going to have animations so you can actually walk through the animation and figure out all the steps. So what I'm going to do in order to simplify this equation is that I'm going to multiply both sides by standard deviation of y so that I can get the rate of the denominator in the left hand side. So what I'll have is y hat minus y bar is equal to r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by. x minus x bar divided by standard deviation of x. Right. my goal is always to have this form, right? In order to get to that form, I just need to move also y bar to the other side of the equation. So what I'll have is that y hat is going to be equal to r times the standard deviation of y divided by a standard deviation of x multiplied by x minus x bar plus y bar. Right. So so far so good. children.title: Deriving the Regression Line Equation
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of the regression line equation, emphasizing the separation of coefficients and constant terms. It explains that the slope, represented as b hat, is equal to the correlation coefficient R multiplied by the standard deviation of Y divided by the standard deviation of X. The relationship indicates that for each standard unit increase in X, Y increases by R times the standard deviation of Y. This derivation connects the concepts of correlation, standard deviation, and the shape of the regression line.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I want to have one term that has all the things or all the coefficients that are multiplied with the variable and every constant term that's going to be added to this core term is going to be separate. So meaning that I want to actually split and take out x from this, parenthesis or this equation right here, which will tell me that why had this going to be equal to our times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x, right? And then the other thing that I'll have is Y bar. Minus are times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x bar. Right? I just rearranged some of the turns. So if we go ahead and take a look at what we had, the coefficient that's multiplied by x is going to be my slope or is going to be my b hat, which that coefficient right here is exactly what we're seeing right here. So this slope is going to be, I'm going to write it here, that this slope is going to be equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. This is what we had seen before. We just gave it to you in data 8 that this slope is equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. of x but this time we actually drive it for you. children.title: Understanding the Intercept in Regression
children.summary: The intercept in a regression equation is defined as y bar minus the slope multiplied by x. This relationship shows that the intercept can be directly calculated using the slope from the regression line equation. The overall regression line equation is also reviewed, emphasizing the components of slope and intercept.

children.title: Regression Line Equation and Residuals
children.summary: The regression line equation is defined as y = a + b*x, where 'a' is the intercept and 'b' is the slope calculated as r * (sy/sx). The residual for the i-th data point is expressed as the difference between the actual value (yi) and the predicted value (ŷi). This segment emphasizes the derivation of the slope and intercept, along with the calculation of residuals.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That the slope is always y bar minus r times y bar minus a slope times x bar. And then obviously, what we have for the residual is that y residual or the error in prediction for data point i is equal to the exact value minus the predicted value y i hat. hat and I use hat because that's a predicted value and that's not the exact value that we calculate. And then why I had you can actually derive it using this equation which now we derive both this lobe and interest of it we can directly have the equation of the line and actually go ahead and calculate it. children.title: Modeling Process and Simple Linear Regression Overview
children.summary: The segment covers the modeling process and regression line review, aiming to derive equations using calculus and evaluate model effectiveness. The regression line is defined as y = a + bx, with the error for each data point expressed as Ci = yi - Gi. Key components include choosing a model, selecting a loss function, fitting the model, and evaluating performance. The modeling process starts with a dataset containing input attributes and a target variable (Y), where each row represents an observation. Simple linear regression involves one input feature predicting a target value, while multiple regression includes several input features. Predictions for observations are denoted as y-hat. The introduction to simple linear regression models emphasizes the equation's intercept and slope, framing models as mathematical functions that describe relationships between independent and dependent variables.

children.title: Modeling Process and Regression Line Review
children.summary: The goal is to derive equations related to the modeling process using calculus and to evaluate model effectiveness. The lecture will cover the regression line equation, defined as y = a + bx, including the slope and intercept. The error for each data point is expressed as Ci = yi - Gi. Key components of the modeling process include choosing a model, selecting a loss function, fitting the model, and evaluating its performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the goal that I have for today's lecture is actually go ahead and derive all of these equations one more time using calculus and then we'll see how we can actually do that. And what's the formal way of us defining how good of a model, how good a model can be and how we can objectively evaluate it. So let's go through the modeling process. So this is the process that we will go over every time that we want to build a model. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Modeling Process in Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The modeling process begins with a dataset containing one or more input attributes and a target variable (Y). Each row in the dataset represents an observation, with inputs (features) used to predict the target variable. In simple linear regression, there is one input feature and one target value, while multiple regression involves multiple input features. Predictions for each observation are denoted as y-hat, with each observation having its own predicted value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Up to the end of the data 100 lecture, every time that I want to talk about creating a new model, I'm going to follow this same modeling process. And let me tell you what this modeling process involves. The first step of the modeling process is that every time that we start doing a modeling, there is going to be a data set. Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat. Video: 1: V

leaf_chunk.audio: Up to the end of the data 100 lecture, every time that I want to talk about creating a new model, I'm going to follow this same modeling process. And let me tell you what this modeling process involves. The first step of the modeling process is that every time that we start doing a modeling, there is going to be a data set. Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression Models
children.summary: The lecture introduces simple linear regression, characterized by an equation that includes an intercept term and a slope multiplied by the input variable. It explains that in Data 100, a model is viewed as a mathematical function that describes relationships between independent and dependent variables. Predictions are denoted as y, with examples of models to be covered in upcoming lectures.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In terms of the models that we will build today and also in the next couple of lectures, we're going to start by building a simple linear regression. So, a simple linear regression is going as having equation like this, that there's always an intercept term, tetasera, and then there's always the slope that we're always referring to that as teta1 times input, and then we will combine the two terms in order to get the production. children.title: Introduction to Parametric Models and Parameter Estimation
children.summary: This segment introduces parametric models, which use parameters learned from data to estimate values. Parameters, denoted as theta, are essential for modeling, while x and y values act as inputs. The goal is to find the optimal parameters (theta hats) that best fit the data through a defined mathematical function. Parametric models are characterized by a limited number of parameters, with optimal values determined by the data. The distinction between estimated parameters (ê) and predicted values is clarified, emphasizing that parameters are informed by the data rather than being predefined. The segment also discusses parameter notation, highlighting how parameters can be organized into vectors for clarity, and contrasts parametric models with non-parametric models, noting that the latter do not have a fixed number of parameters.

children.title: Introduction to Parametric Models and Parameters
children.summary: The discussion focuses on parametric models, which utilize parameters learned from data to estimate values. The parameters, denoted as theta, are crucial for modeling, while x and y values serve as inputs or features. The optimum set of parameters, represented as theta hats, is essential for accurately modeling the dataset. The segment emphasizes that defining a parametric model involves establishing a mathematical function, but finding the optimal function that best fits the data is the goal.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function. You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have.
