leaf_chunk.audio: We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function. You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have.

children.title: Parametric Models and Parameter Estimation
children.summary: Parametric models in data analysis are defined by a limited number of parameters, such as theta values (θ0, θ1). The optimal parameters for a model are determined by the data rather than being predefined. In this context, x and y values are not parameters since they are directly observed. The estimated parameter, denoted as ê, is distinct from the predicted value, which is also represented with a hat. The selection of parameters aims to achieve the best fit according to a chosen criterion, leading to a linear model characterized by parameters θ = [θ0, θ1].

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, this right here is going to be a family of the models that can be applied to our data set, but there is only one set of values that is tetA0 hadn't tetA1 had that are going to be optimal. Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector. Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector.

children.title: Parametric Models and Parameter Notation
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by a limited set of parameters, such as theta zero and theta one, which can be combined into a vector for easier management and indexing. This notation allows for a clearer understanding of the relationship between features and their corresponding weights. The parameters are not provided directly; instead, they are informed by the data. The sample-based estimate of a parameter is denoted as ê, distinguishing it from predictions. The best fitting linear model is chosen based on specific criteria.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, in this case, what I've done is that I'm saying that my model uses theta zero and theta one. Let's combine them into a vector of theta. And you pass me theta or you pass me theta hat. I know what model you're using. I know what parameters you're using, and I can go ahead and use those parameters, right? Instead of just go to a, b, c, d, and so on and so forth. this way just using the subscript of i, tetasero, tetah1, tetah2, and so on and so forth. What we can do is we can consolidate them into a tetavector and then we can index into the relationship between each feature has what weight more accurately or more easily. So that's kind of the notation that we'll be using. Video: 1: lec10.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.title: Parametric vs Non-parametric Models in Statistics
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by a limited number of parameters (e.g., β0, β1), which are informed by the data rather than predetermined. In contrast, non-parametric models, such as Kernel Density Estimates (KDE) and k-Nearest Neighbors, do not require parameter setting. The x and y values observed in the data are not considered parameters. An estimated parameter is denoted as ê, which differs from the prediction notation in y. The best fitting linear model can be expressed as y = β0 + β1x, highlighting the importance of parameter estimation in parametric models.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I said here we have a parametric model. We have seen non parametric models before. So some statistical models do not have parameters. For example, we have seen KDE's. KDE's were models, or kernel density estimate. But we did not set any parameters. We would just estimate alpha, and then we would just run a process, and we would have KDE's. Kairi's neighbors is also another model that you did not need to set parameters, or there was not no parameter to learn. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Introduction to Parametric Models and Their Estimation
children.summary: Parametric models are characterized by a limited number of parameters, which are informed by the data rather than being provided explicitly. The segment discusses the process of learning and deriving these parameters to create a unique model. It highlights the distinction between observed values (x, y) and estimated parameters, with the notation for estimates (ê) being clarified. The segment also notes that not all statistical models have parameters, contrasting parametric models with non-parametric models like KDEs and k-Nearest Neighbor classifiers. The initial step in the modeling process is to choose an appropriate model that represents the world.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So those are the cases that there are models that are non-parametric, there are models that are parametric, but we're dealing with is parametric models. And what we're going to do throughout this multiple lecture is to learn those parameters, how to optimally learn those parameters, how to derive those parameters so that we can uniquely have our own model to work with. So the terminology and we know the notation that we'll be using, let me walk you through the modeling process. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Steps in the Modeling Process
children.summary: The first step in building a model is to choose the category of model, such as simple linear regression for this lecture. This choice sets the framework for the modeling process. In subsequent lectures, different types of models may be explored. Additionally, the process involves selecting a loss function to quantify prediction error.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Every time that we want to build a model, irrespective of what model you're building, you first need to choose the category of model that you want to use. So for example, for today's lecture, what we would say is that our model is going to be a simple linear regression. We might change our mind in the next few lectures, and we will keep using different types of models. But the first step is that you figure out what framework you want to use. children.title: Loss Function Selection and Model Evaluation
children.summary: A loss function is defined as a tool to evaluate the accuracy of predictions made by a model. The modeling process involves selecting a model, choosing a loss function to quantify prediction error, fitting the model to determine the best parameters, and evaluating the model's performance. Optimizing the loss function includes finding the best parameters, transitioning from TETA to TETA hats. The evaluation criteria focus on assessing model performance, using simple linear regression as an example. Key terms like the theta vector are introduced, which represents a family of functions used to obtain predictions. The segment further explains estimating parameters in simple linear regression, denoting predictions with a hat, and optimizing parameters to provide the best estimations for the target variable y, calculated using the formula y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 * x.

children.title: Choosing a Loss Function in Modeling
children.summary: A loss function is defined as a tool to evaluate the accuracy of predictions made by a model. The next steps in the modeling process include selecting a model to represent the world, choosing a loss function to quantify prediction error, and fitting the model to determine the best parameters based on the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The second step is to choose a loss function. What is a loss function? Loss function is a function that will tell you how good your bad your predictions are. And I'm going to objectively define what this loss function means and how to choose a loss function in the next few slides. Once we have the model and we have the loss function, we'll go through an optimization process. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling X

children.title: Optimizing Loss Function and Model Evaluation
children.summary: The process of optimizing the loss function involves finding the best set of parameters for the model, transitioning from TETA to TETA hats. Once the model is fitted and the optimal parameters are determined, the next step is to evaluate the model's performance. The modeling process includes choosing a model, selecting a loss function to quantify prediction error, fitting the model to determine the best parameters, and finally evaluating the model to assess its effectiveness.

Audio: This is a fun part that we'll actually go ahead and try to optimize our loss function in a way that we find the best set of parameters for our model. children.title: Model Selection and Evaluation Criteria
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the evaluation criteria for assessing model performance, specifically using simple linear regression as the chosen model. Key terms and notations are introduced, including the use of the theta vector, which represents a family of functions. The process involves passing input values to the theta vector to obtain predictions. The modeling process is outlined with four main steps: choosing a model, selecting a loss function to quantify prediction error, fitting the model to determine the best parameters, and evaluating the model's performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're going to briefly talk about whether the evaluation criteria that we'll have after we have the model to make sure that our model is performing well on the data set that we have. So let's start with choosing a model, which should be simple in this case. We're choosing simple linear regression for the purpose of today's lecture, because today's lecture is just basically setting you up with all the new notation and the new terminology that we're using. We're going from AMB, we're going into the word of TETA, so we're using TETA 0 as my Y interest of and TETA 1 as this loop that we're using. So what I said was that this tetah vector that we have is the finding a family of functions. So what that means is that if you are saying that you have a tetah vector and you pass the values of tetasurant, tetah one of that tetah vector, you pass the input, you have the predictions. So think about that as a machine that throughout the modeling process will try to find the parameters for this machine that gives us the best estimations, right? children.title: Understanding Simple Linear Regression and Loss Function
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of estimating parameters in simple linear regression, denoted with a hat to indicate predictions. It explains that the parameters (theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat) are optimized to provide the best estimations for the target variable y, calculated using the formula y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 * x. The focus then shifts to defining the loss function, which quantifies the accuracy of the model's predictions against the actual data. The segment also illustrates the concept with three lines representing different sets of parameters in the theta vector.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's what it means. What we have is that the parameters are sitting in the middle and we're trying to find the optimums that are parameters in the middle to get the best estimations, the best estimations as much as possible. So one thing to notice here is that I'm using also hat on top of the estimations. And I was using hat one more time when I was putting it on top of tetas. This is kind of operate or overloading that I'm using. So when it happens on top of why that means that's an estimation. When hat is on top of, or that's the prediction. When hat is on top of the teta, that means that that's the optimum set of parameters for the model that I'm using. The first step is done. We have a linear, simple linear regression. We know that the pattern of the function is going to be that two parameters are going to be given, and then we can calculate the prediction of y, which is y hat, using teta 0 plus theta 1 times x. Now we need to get into what is the loss function? And how can we define how good we are matching to the target values based on different models that we have? So we are going to actually follow a certain process that defining a cost function and optimizing the cost function up to the point that we get to the optimum set of parameters that I'm referring to as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. So choosing a loss function, how can we quantify how good or bad a function is map to our data set? So for example, what you're seeing here is that I have tree lines. Again, remember that that machine in the middle was defining a family of lines. So here in this case, I'm just showing out tree random kind of sets of values for the the tetah vector. leaf_chunk.audio: So that's what it means. What we have is that the parameters are sitting in the middle and we're trying to find the optimums that are parameters in the middle to get the best estimations, the best estimations as much as possible. So one thing to notice here is that I'm using also hat on top of the estimations. And I was using hat one more time when I was putting it on top of tetas. This is kind of operate or overloading that I'm using. So when it happens on top of why that means that's an estimation. When hat is on top of, or that's the prediction. When hat is on top of the teta, that means that that's the optimum set of parameters for the model that I'm using. The first step is done. We have a linear, simple linear regression. We know that the pattern of the function is going to be that two parameters are going to be given, and then we can calculate the prediction of y, which is y hat, using teta 0 plus theta 1 times x. Now we need to get into what is the loss function? And how can we define how good we are matching to the target values based on different models that we have? So we are going to actually follow a certain process that defining a cost function and optimizing the cost function up to the point that we get to the optimum set of parameters that I'm referring to as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. So choosing a loss function, how can we quantify how good or bad a function is map to our data set? So for example, what you're seeing here is that I have tree lines. Again, remember that that machine in the middle was defining a family of lines. So here in this case, I'm just showing out tree random kind of sets of values for the the tetah vector. So here, tetah 0, tetah 1, tetah 0, tetah 1 for three separate lines.

children.title: Evaluating Model Accuracy with Data Distribution
children.summary: The discussion focuses on assessing the accuracy of different linear models based on their fit to a given data set. Participants are prompted to consider which model line aligns best with the data distribution and to define what accuracy means in this context. The slide presents a scenario involving the estimation of the number of characters in chapters of the novel 'Little Women' based on the number of periods, encouraging engagement through an active poll on the optimal parameters for the linear model.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Modeling
children.summary: The blue line in the data set demonstrates a better fit compared to the red line, as it minimizes the average distance from the data points, resulting in lower error. This leads to the introduction of loss functions, which serve as a metric to evaluate the accuracy of predictions. A loss function quantifies the error for a single observation, indicating that lower loss is desirable when the prediction is close to the actual value, while higher loss is expected when the prediction is further from the actual value.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Modeling
children.summary: The loss function quantifies the accuracy of predictions by measuring the difference between predicted values (y hat) and actual values (y). A small loss indicates predictions are close to actual values, while a high loss shows significant discrepancies. The choice of loss function is vital as it affects how the model is penalized for errors. Various types of loss functions will be discussed, each indicating prediction quality and differing in penalization based on the distance from actual values.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Modeling
children.summary: The loss function measures the accuracy of predictions by quantifying the difference between predicted values (y hat) and actual values (y). A small loss indicates that predictions are close to the actual values, while a high loss signifies a significant discrepancy. The choice of loss function is crucial, as it determines how the model is penalized for errors. Different types of loss functions will be explored in the course, each serving to indicate the quality of predictions and varying in their penalization based on the distance from actual values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're defining the loss function in the same way. We would say that we're going to take a look at the prediction predicted values. We're going to take a look at the real values. And we're going to have a single up to single value that assigned to how bad the predictions are. So what we would say is that if y hat is close to y, we would give you a little loss. law. So we would say that you did not do that badly and then we would give a value in our, which is a small value. But if a Y hat is far from Y, in the cases that the predictions are delined was far from the actual individual values, we would give a high loss. That's the general idea. Her given data point, we would calculate loss, we would calculate the error, and we need to have a function that will actually penalize the model based on each one of those miscalculations or mis-systemations that it has. So the choice of the loss function is really important. In data 100, we're going to cover, I think, three or four different types of loss functions that will actually do exactly the same thing. It will tell us how bad our predictions were, and it will basically tell us that it will penalize us more if our prediction is far from the actual value, and it will penalize us less if our prediction is close to the actual target value. But the choice of the loss function really depends on multiple different metrics and different types of modeling that you're doing. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

leaf_chunk.audio: So we're defining the loss function in the same way. We would say that we're going to take a look at the prediction predicted values. We're going to take a look at the real values. And we're going to have a single up to single value that assigned to how bad the predictions are. So what we would say is that if y hat is close to y, we would give you a little loss. law. So we would say that you did not do that badly and then we would give a value in our, which is a small value. But if a Y hat is far from Y, in the cases that the predictions are delined was far from the actual individual values, we would give a high loss. That's the general idea. Her given data point, we would calculate loss, we would calculate the error, and we need to have a function that will actually penalize the model based on each one of those miscalculations or mis-systemations that it has. So the choice of the loss function is really important. In data 100, we're going to cover, I think, three or four different types of loss functions that will actually do exactly the same thing. It will tell us how bad our predictions were, and it will basically tell us that it will penalize us more if our prediction is far from the actual value, and it will penalize us less if our prediction is close to the actual target value. But the choice of the loss function really depends on multiple different metrics and different types of modeling that you're doing.

children.title: Loss Functions in Classification and Regression
children.summary: The choice of loss function differs between classification and regression tasks, impacting model accuracy and computational efficiency. In classification tasks, such as cancer identification, minimizing missed cases is critical, leading to the use of specific loss functions. Two common loss functions are L2 loss (squared loss), which calculates the residual by subtracting the predicted value from the actual value, and L1 loss (absolute loss), which quantifies prediction error differently. The selection of a loss function should align with the specific estimation task.

children.title: Loss Functions in Classification and Regression
children.summary: The choice of loss function varies significantly between classification and regression tasks. For instance, in classification tasks like cancer identification, it's crucial to minimize missed cases, necessitating specific loss functions. Two common loss functions are introduced: L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss). L2 loss calculates the residual by subtracting the predicted value from the actual value and scoring this residual, while L1 loss also quantifies prediction error but in a different manner. The selection of a loss function influences model accuracy and computational efficiency, and it should align with the specific estimation task.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: For example, if you're doing a classification task versus a regression task, the loss function is going to be very different. If you're doing a classification task, for example, if you're doing cancer identification in a sense, you really don't want to miss any potential cancer patients. So in those cases, you really want to have a different kinds of loss function that accounts for all of those cases as well. So we're going to take a look at all of those considerations when we go into evaluating the models a little bit more, or just putting it out there that when I talk about loss function today, I'm going to introduce two loss functions, but those are not the only loss functions that we use. Depending on the use case, the loss function can be different. So, last function of the day, you might have seen these equations before. One last function is called L2 loss or a squirt loss. One loss is called absolute loss or L1 loss, order 1, order 2. Right? Think about them like that. So the squirt loss basically says that go ahead and take a look at the actual value, subtract the predicted value, so meaning that calculate the residual, and go ahead and score the residual. children.title: L1 and L2 Loss Functions in Regression
children.summary: The segment explains L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss) in error scoring, noting that smaller residuals incur less penalty than larger ones, with examples illustrating the differences in penalties. It discusses their application in linear regression, detailing how L2 loss penalizes the square of the residual while L1 loss uses the absolute value, emphasizing the importance of averaging these losses for model fitting. A comparison of the two loss functions highlights their effectiveness in penalizing deviations, with a poll engaging participants on the suitability of each function. The segment also addresses squirt loss as a method for penalizing large miscalculations, discussing its advantages and potential drawbacks.

children.title: Understanding L2 and L1 Loss Functions
children.summary: The segment discusses the concepts of L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss) in error scoring. It explains how small residuals are penalized less compared to larger residuals, with specific examples illustrating the differences in penalties. For instance, a small residual value results in a smaller penalty, while a large residual value, such as 100 units, leads to a significantly larger penalty of 10,000 when squared. The importance of these loss functions in evaluating predictions and their impact on model fitting is emphasized.

Compared to the case that, okay, If you have 100 units of residual, it will square it to penalize you even more. So the penalty right now is going to be 10,000 in that case. children.title: L1 and L2 Loss in Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss) in the context of linear regression. L2 loss penalizes the square of the residual, while L1 loss considers the absolute value of the residual, ensuring that the loss is always positive. The equations for both losses are presented, where y hat represents the predicted value from the simple linear regression model. The focus is on how to calculate the loss for individual data points and the importance of averaging these losses across all data points to achieve a line that fits the dataset closely.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's kind of L2 loss or the second order loss that we have. The absolute loss just looks at the residual and calculus the absolute value. Again, meaning that if it's the smallest, it's just going to be that same absolute value of the residual. If it's 100 unit of residual, it's just going to be same 100 units. It's not going to be penalized any further. But in both of the cases, they're penalizing. And in both of the cases, what you notice is that the loss is always a positive value because it's a cost. So we want to give a cost. We don't want to give a negative cost. So in the cost in both of the cases, in our case, is positive. So what does it mean in the... in the language of linear regression, that means that I'm going to go ahead and replace in every instance that I have y hat, I'm going to replace it with the equation for the simple linear regression. So here I have y minus y hat, so here my loss for simple linear regression based on L2 or a squared loss is going to be y minus this right here is y hat and then the squared. Same thing for the L1 loss, we just plug in y hat and then we have the equation. Note to step what I have right here is the loss or the cost or a single data point. But what we want is we want to have a line that matches all of the data sets, all of the data points in the data set as closely as possible. So in that case, what we would do is we would go ahead and take average over all of the data points. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

leaf_chunk.audio: So that's kind of L2 loss or the second order loss that we have. The absolute loss just looks at the residual and calculus the absolute value. Again, meaning that if it's the smallest, it's just going to be that same absolute value of the residual. If it's 100 unit of residual, it's just going to be same 100 units. It's not going to be penalized any further. But in both of the cases, they're penalizing. And in both of the cases, what you notice is that the loss is always a positive value because it's a cost. So we want to give a cost. We don't want to give a negative cost. So in the cost in both of the cases, in our case, is positive. So what does it mean in the... in the language of linear regression, that means that I'm going to go ahead and replace in every instance that I have y hat, I'm going to replace it with the equation for the simple linear regression. So here I have y minus y hat, so here my loss for simple linear regression based on L2 or a squared loss is going to be y minus this right here is y hat and then the squared. Same thing for the L1 loss, we just plug in y hat and then we have the equation. Note to step what I have right here is the loss or the cost or a single data point. But what we want is we want to have a line that matches all of the data sets, all of the data points in the data set as closely as possible. So in that case, what we would do is we would go ahead and take average over all of the data points.

children.title: Choosing Loss Functions for Penalizing Deviations
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the use of squirt loss as an effective method for penalizing large miscalculations or mispredictions. It highlights that squirt loss is ideal for situations where significant deviations need to be heavily penalized. However, it also mentions a drawback of this approach, indicating that while penalizing large residuals is beneficial, it may have negative implications.

children.title: Impact of Outliers on Loss Functions
children.summary: Outliers can significantly affect model performance as they tend to deviate from the main trend of the data. To mitigate the influence of large residuals, models may adjust the trend line to balance between minimizing loss on the actual data trend and addressing the outlier points. The discussion also includes a poll regarding the ideal loss function for penalizing large residuals more than small ones, highlighting the preference for squared loss over absolute loss.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Just a quick hint would be an outlier. Anything about outliers might be an issue here. Yeah. So the answer was that the outliers are generally far from the trend of the data. And the model, if model wants to try to avoid having that squared large loss that it might incur, it might tilt the line or the model from the actual trend that it needs to be to the trend that's kind of have meets halfway in between. So that it gets some small loss on the actual trend that the data and also gets closer to the outlier data point that it has. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Importance of Absolute and Squared Loss
children.summary: The discussion focuses on why residual values are not used directly in calculations. It emphasizes the need to utilize absolute loss or squared loss to avoid the cancellation of positive and negative residuals, which would result in a sum of zero. The necessity of transforming residuals into positive values is highlighted to ensure meaningful distance measurements in error analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What about this question? Why don't we use the actual residual values directly? Why do we score them? Why do we calculate absolute value? Why do we want all of them to be all of the cost values to be positive? Why do we want to get rid of negatives? Sum will be zero. Residual sum to zero, that is correct. Um... Okay, they don't cancel out. So that seems to be the right trend. But the issue that we have when we try to just use the actual residual values is that some residual values are positive, some residual values are negative, right? children.title: Using Absolute and Squared Loss Functions
children.summary: Residual errors can cancel each other out, leading to a sum of zero, which is problematic for cost calculations. To avoid this, it is essential to use absolute loss or squared loss functions, as they ensure all values are positive, allowing for proper standardization and comparison of costs. This approach prevents large negative residuals from offsetting large positive ones, ensuring that the model's predictions are accurately assessed.

children.title: Calculating Average Loss for Model Performance
children.summary: The lecture discusses the importance of using absolute loss or squared loss to evaluate model performance across all data points. It explains that the total loss is calculated by summing the loss values for each data point and then dividing by the number of data points (n) to obtain the average loss. This approach ensures that the size of the datasets does not skew comparisons between model performances. Additionally, it addresses why residual error alone is not a suitable loss function, as large negative and positive residuals can cancel each other out, misleadingly suggesting a zero residual when predictions may still be significantly off.

leaf_chunk.text:

children.title: Empirical Risk, Cost Functions, and Regression Fitting
children.summary: The segment covers empirical risk and its mathematical representation as R(θ) = (1/n) * Σ L(yi, ŷi), emphasizing the impact of varying theta values on predictions and average loss. It introduces L2 loss, specifically mean squared error (MSE), and L1 loss for assessing model accuracy. The discussion includes fitting a Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model by optimizing parameters theta 0 and theta 1 to minimize MSE, detailing the process of calculating derivatives and setting them to zero for optimality. The importance of partial derivatives in MSE minimization is highlighted, along with the application of the chain rule in deriving these derivatives.

children.title: Empirical Risk, Cost Functions, and Loss Types
children.summary: The segment discusses the role of parametric functions in modeling, defined by theta, where different theta values yield different predictions (y-hats). Empirical risk is introduced as the average loss across the dataset, mathematically represented as R(θ) = (1/n) * Σ L(yi, ŷi), with L being the loss function. It emphasizes how varying predicted values affect the average loss or cost function, aiming to minimize this cost for better model fit. The cost function is influenced by theta, with changes in theta altering predictions and costs. Specifically highlighted is empirical risk as mean squared error using L2 loss or average absolute residuals with L1 loss for evaluating model prediction accuracy. The segment also covers L2 loss, particularly mean squared error (MSE), in simple linear regression, explaining that the empirical risk function evaluates model performance across all data points. It introduces 'least squares regression' focused on minimizing squared prediction errors and contrasts L2 loss with L1 loss, noting that different loss functions can lead to different final models.

children.title: Empirical Risk, Cost Functions, and Model Parameters
children.summary: The discussion covers the role of parametric functions in modeling, defined by theta, where different theta values yield different predictions (y-hats). Empirical risk is introduced as the average loss across the dataset, represented mathematically as R(θ) = (1/n) * Σ L(yi, ŷi), with L being the loss function. The segment emphasizes how varying predicted values affect the average loss or cost function, aiming to minimize this cost to ensure the model fits the data closely. The cost function is influenced by theta, with changes in theta altering predictions and costs. Empirical risk, defined as the average loss, is specifically highlighted as mean squared error using L2 loss or average absolute residuals with L1 loss, assisting in evaluating model prediction accuracy.

children.title: Empirical Risk and Parametric Functions in Modeling
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the function used to train a model, emphasizing that it is a parametric function defined by theta. Different theta values yield different predictions (y-hats). Additionally, the concept of empirical risk is introduced as the average loss across the entire dataset, which is crucial for evaluating model performance. The empirical risk is mathematically represented as R(0) = (1/n) * Σ L(yi, ŷi), where L represents the loss function and D is the dataset.

children.title: Empirical Risk and Average Loss Calculation
children.summary: The segment discusses how different predicted values (y-hats) lead to varying losses for data points, which in turn affects the overall average loss or cost function. The objective is to find the parameter values (theta zero and theta one) that minimize this cost function, ensuring the model closely fits the data on average with minimal residual values. It emphasizes the importance of empirical risk, defined as the average loss across all data points, to assess the model's prediction accuracy. The formula for empirical risk is presented, highlighting its dependence on the model parameters while holding the data constant.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And by having different y-hats, you're going to have different losses for the data points. And then once you have different losses, you're going to have a different overall cost or the overall average loss. So, kind of notice that this function, depending on the teta is going to change, and our goal in this case is going to be to find the teta value or the set of teta zero and teta one values that minimizes this cost function that goes through all of the data points as closely as possible on average and have the minimum average residual values that we have. children.title: Understanding Cost Functions and Empirical Risk
children.summary: The cost function is crucial as it is parametrized by TETA, which influences the model and subsequently the cost on the dataset. Changing the TETA value alters the predictions and thus the cost function. The empirical risk, representing the average loss across the dataset, is defined as the mean squared error when using L2 loss, calculated as 1 over N times the summation of the squared differences between actual and predicted values. Alternatively, using L1 loss results in the average of the absolute residuals. This average loss helps assess how well the model fits the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, does that make sense? So this cost function is a really important cost function. Again, it's parametrized by TETA, meaning that depending on the TETA value, the value of the cost on the data set is going to change. And why is it going to change? Because when you change the TETA value, you're effectively changing the model. When you're changing the model, your cost function is going to be different because your predictions are going to be different. So think about this function as a function that evaluates the entire family of the lines that can pass through your data set. So when we go ahead and plug in for that loss function and plug in L2 loss, you can see that effectively the empirical risk is going to be 1 over N times the summation of Yi minus Yi i2, and we call that mean a squared error. And if you plug in L1 loss instead of this loss function into the empirical risk equation, this is going to give us 1 over N times the summation of absolute value of all of those residual values that we have. leaf_chunk.audio: Okay, does that make sense? So this cost function is a really important cost function. Again, it's parametrized by TETA, meaning that depending on the TETA value, the value of the cost on the data set is going to change. And why is it going to change? Because when you change the TETA value, you're effectively changing the model. When you're changing the model, your cost function is going to be different because your predictions are going to be different. So think about this function as a function that evaluates the entire family of the lines that can pass through your data set. So when we go ahead and plug in for that loss function and plug in L2 loss, you can see that effectively the empirical risk is going to be 1 over N times the summation of Yi minus Yi i2, and we call that mean a squared error. And if you plug in L1 loss instead of this loss function into the empirical risk equation, this is going to give us 1 over N times the summation of absolute value of all of those residual values that we have.

children.title: L2 Loss and Mean Squared Error in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses L2 loss, specifically mean squared error (MSE), in simple linear regression. It explains that the empirical risk function is the average loss over the dataset, which evaluates model performance across all data points. The concept of 'least squares regression' is introduced, focusing on minimizing squared prediction errors. Additionally, it contrasts L2 loss with L1 loss, indicating that different loss functions can result in different final models.

children.title: L2 Loss and Mean Squared Error in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of L2 loss, specifically mean squared error (MSE), in the context of simple linear regression. It explains that the empirical risk function is calculated as the average loss over the dataset, emphasizing that the model's performance is evaluated across all data points. The term 'least squares regression' is introduced, highlighting that this approach aims to minimize the squared errors of predictions. The segment also contrasts L2 loss with L1 loss, noting that different loss functions can lead to different final models.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, MSc versus MAE. In the next lecture, I'm going to come back and play with optimizing both of these functions. And I'm going to show you what's the difference between the final model that we'll get depending on which loss function that we use and which empirical response we get at the end. For today's purpose, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say that I'm going to use L2 loss, meaning that I'm going to go ahead and calculate the residual square of each one of those data points in the dataset. And my entire empirical risk function is going to be MSE or mean squared error. Again, this is the mean and this is the squared error of all of those predictions that we have. This is YI, this is YI hat that we have based on the simple linear regression. What you know from data 8 is that you know that they called this delinear regression that they were teaching you, they called it least the squares regression. We also used exactly the same terminology here. Effectively, simple linear regression pattern or function together with the optimization function that is mean squared error is called least the scores regression because we're trying to find a function that minimizes these scores of the errors that we have over the entire population. leaf_chunk.audio: So, MSc versus MAE. In the next lecture, I'm going to come back and play with optimizing both of these functions. And I'm going to show you what's the difference between the final model that we'll get depending on which loss function that we use and which empirical response we get at the end. For today's purpose, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say that I'm going to use L2 loss, meaning that I'm going to go ahead and calculate the residual square of each one of those data points in the dataset. And my entire empirical risk function is going to be MSE or mean squared error. Again, this is the mean and this is the squared error of all of those predictions that we have. This is YI, this is YI hat that we have based on the simple linear regression. What you know from data 8 is that you know that they called this delinear regression that they were teaching you, they called it least the squares regression. We also used exactly the same terminology here. Effectively, simple linear regression pattern or function together with the optimization function that is mean squared error is called least the scores regression because we're trying to find a function that minimizes these scores of the errors that we have over the entire population.

children.title: Fitting and Minimizing Simple Linear Regression Models
children.summary: Fitting a Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model involves optimizing parameters theta 0 and theta 1 to minimize the mean squared error (MSE). This includes calculating the derivatives of the MSE function with respect to these parameters and setting them to zero for optimal points. The segment highlights the importance of deriving partial derivatives for MSE minimization, connecting it to prior math knowledge. MSE is defined as the average of squared residuals (y_i - y_i hat)², and the process of taking derivatives of summations is explained, noting that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives. The application of the chain rule in deriving the partial derivative of MSE is also covered.

children.title: Fitting and Minimizing Simple Linear Regression Models
children.summary: Fitting a Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model involves optimizing parameters theta 0 and theta 1 to minimize the mean squared error (MSE). This requires calculating the derivatives of the MSE function with respect to these parameters and setting them to zero to find optimal points. The segment emphasizes the importance of deriving partial derivatives for MSE minimization, linking it to previous math courses. The MSE is calculated as the average of squared residuals (y_i - y_i hat)², and the process of taking derivatives of summations is explained, highlighting that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives. The application of the chain rule in deriving the partial derivative of MSE is also discussed.

children.title: Fitting Simple Linear Regression Models
children.summary: Fitting a model involves finding optimal values for parameters theta 0 and theta 1 that minimize the cost function, specifically the mean squared error (MSE). This requires calculating the derivative of the MSE function with respect to both parameters, setting these derivatives to zero to find the optimum points. The process is part of least squares regression, which combines a chosen model with a loss function to evaluate performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now down to the fun part. I said that if you have the two parts in the model, you have the model kind of Hadron or the template which in this case is simple linear regression and you know what kind of cost function you're using Then everything else is going to be easy fitting the model is going to be easy What I'll do is I'll basically tell you that what does it mean to fit the model when we say that we want to fit the model That means that I want to find a combination of theta 0 and theta 1 values that are giving me the minimum function or the minimum value for the right hand side of this msc function So in order to do so, what you know from your math courses is that I need to calculate the derivative of this function with respect to theta 0, set it to 0 with respect to theta 1, set it equal to 0, find those optimum points, and those optimum points, theta 0 and theta 1 are going to be exactly my theta 0 had on my theta 1 hat that I can use. children.title: Calculating Partial Derivatives for MSE Minimization
children.summary: The upcoming segment will focus on deriving partial derivatives, which is essential for understanding the optimization process in machine learning. It emphasizes the importance of prior math courses in applying these concepts effectively. The goal is to minimize the Mean Squared Error (MSE) in the Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model by setting the derivatives equal to zero to find optimal values.

children.title: Minimizing Mean Squared Error in SLR Model
children.summary: The discussion focuses on minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE) for the Simple Linear Regression (SLR) model. The objective function calculates the average of the squared residuals, expressed as (y_i - y_i hat)². To find the optimal values for the regression coefficients, the derivatives of the MSE with respect to these coefficients are set to zero, leading to the optimality conditions necessary for minimizing the error.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because Machine learning is all about having various strong calculus and linear algebra background and this is this is the place that you will see where it will become handy. So to start by taking partial derivatives. So moving that equation right over here. So It says audio is out. I don't think it's up. It's on. Okay cool. So, I have the objective function and let me reiterate what the subjective function was. Remember that the subjective function is calculating the average. over the scores of the errors. Right? So this is the error inside the parentheses. What you're seeing is y i minus y i hat, which is the residual to the power of two that I'm calculating, which is mean a scored error that I want to calculate. So I'm going to take the derivative with respect to tetasera, which effectively what that means is that I'll do the normal derivative, but I'm just assuming that anything except for tetasera is going to be constant value, right? children.title: Taking Derivative of Summation in MSE
children.summary: The process of taking the derivative of a summation term is discussed, emphasizing that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives. The speaker outlines the steps involved, specifically extracting the coefficient and summation for clarity. The focus is on deriving with respect to theta zero, where the expression involves the difference between observed values and the model prediction squared.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And there are a couple of steps that I need to do first. One is that I'm taking the derivative of a summation term, right? And we know that the derivative of sum is equal to sum of the derivatives. So I'm going to take out the coefficient and the summation out of the derivatives so that things are a little bit easier and cleaner. So moving this summation out is is going to give me 1 over n times the summation of pi from 1 up to n of the derivative of what I had, which is yi minus theta zero. minus the one x i. children.title: Applying Chain Rule for MSE Derivative
children.summary: The segment discusses applying the chain rule to derive the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to parameters θ0 and θ1. It explains taking the derivative of the outer function and the inner function, specifically focusing on the term involving θ0. The derivative of the function is expressed as a function of the differences between observed values (yi) and predicted values (θ0 + θ1xi), leading to the formulation of the MSE and its derivative.

children.title: Partial Derivatives of MSE for Theta Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta zero and theta one. It details that the derivative with respect to theta zero yields a negative constant multiplied by the sum of the residuals (yi - theta0 - theta1 * xi). For theta one, the derivative employs the chain rule, resulting in a factor of two times the function and its derivative. Both derivatives are set to zero to find the optimal parameters, emphasizing that at the optimum, the sum of the residuals equals zero for theta zero, and the sum of residuals multiplied by x_i equals zero, highlighting the connection between residuals and parameters.

children.title: Partial Derivatives of MSE for Theta Parameters
children.summary: The segment covers the process of taking partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) concerning the parameters theta zero and theta one. It explains that the derivative with respect to theta zero results in a negative constant term multiplied by the summation of the residuals (yi - theta0 - theta1 * xi). For theta one, the derivative involves the chain rule, leading to a factor of two times the function and its derivative. Both derivatives are set to zero to solve for the parameters. The discussion also emphasizes that at the optimum point, the summation of the residuals must equal zero for theta zero, and the summation of residuals multiplied by x_i must also equal zero, illustrating the relationship between the residuals and the parameters.

children.title: Partial Derivatives of MSE for Theta Parameters
children.summary: The process of taking partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta zero and theta one is discussed. The derivative with respect to theta zero results in a negative constant term multiplied by the summation of the residuals (yi - theta0 - theta1 * xi). For theta one, the derivative involves applying the chain rule, where the function is squared, leading to a factor of two times the function and the derivative of the function with respect to theta one. Both derivatives are set to zero to solve for the parameters.

children.title: Applying Chain Rule to MSE Derivative
children.summary: The segment discusses applying the chain rule to find the derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta zero and theta one. It details the process of taking the derivative of the residual function, leading to the conclusion that the summation of the residuals should equal zero at the optimum point for theta zero. Additionally, it emphasizes that the summation of residuals multiplied by x_i should also equal zero, highlighting the relationship between the residuals and the parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um, so I'm going to apply the chain rule exactly. So one over n times the summation of i from one up to n of the function y i minus theta zero minus theta one x i. Now the derivative. So what is the derivative of that green term with respect to theta one? Yes. Negative x i is the derivative, right? So negative x i is my derivative right here. So I'm just going to go ahead simplify constants one more time. So here is going to give me, sorry, I missed it too. So it's two times f times whatever. So this is going to be negative 2 over n of the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus minus theta zero minus theta one x i times x i. Right. Look at both sides. Look at that function f thing that's in common between them. So one is effectively telling me that if I go ahead and set this to be equal to zero, one, the left hand side is effectively telling me that the summation of all of the residuals should be equal to zero. Because that's residual. And that's why they were canceling each other out. That's why they couldn't be a cost function. Right? This is telling me that at the point that theta zero is optimum, the summation of residuals should be equal to zero. And then the right hand side is basically telling me that The summation of residuals times x i's should be equal to zero. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Optimal Theta Values and Estimating Equations Derivation
children.summary: The segment details the process of solving for optimal theta values (theta zero hat and theta one hat) using two equations set to zero, focusing on the summation of residuals and the partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE). It emphasizes the application of the chain rule in derivation and the importance of these derivatives in understanding MSE. The segment presents estimating equations to find optimal parameters, ensuring the difference between observed and predicted values equals zero and relating residuals to independent variables. It simplifies expressions to derive theta zero hat as y bar minus the slope times x bar, establishing the objective function. The process for finding theta one hat involves combining estimating equations and using algebraic methods to isolate theta one hat. Finally, it discusses deriving the optimum estimate for y (y hat) through substitution and simplification.

children.title: Optimal Theta Values and Estimating Equations
children.summary: The segment covers the process of solving for optimum theta values (theta zero hat and theta one hat) by introducing two equations set to zero, focusing on the summation of residuals and the partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE). It discusses the application of the chain rule in the derivation, highlighting the significance of these derivatives for understanding MSE. Estimating equations are presented to find optimal parameters, with one ensuring the difference between observed and predicted values equals zero and the other relating residuals to independent variables. The derivation of these estimating equations involves simplifying expressions related to observed values and parameters, aiming to satisfy conditions for optimal parameter estimation.

children.title: Solving for Optimum Theta Values
children.summary: Two equations with unknowns theta zero and theta one are introduced, which are set to zero to find their optimum values, denoted as theta zero hat and theta one hat. The segment discusses the equations related to the summation of residuals equating to zero and the partial derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to theta zero and theta one. The chain rule is applied in the derivation process, and simplifications are noted, emphasizing the importance of understanding these derivatives in the context of the MSE.

children.title: Estimating Equations and Optimality Conditions
children.summary: Estimating equations are used to find optimal values for parameters theta i. The process involves setting the derivatives of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) to zero to establish optimality conditions. Two key estimating equations are presented: the first ensures that the difference between observed values (yi) and predicted values (Ûi) equals zero, while the second incorporates the relationship between the residuals and the independent variables (xi) to also equal zero. The goal is to solve these equations to determine the best estimates for the parameters.

children.title: Deriving Estimating Equations
children.summary: The segment focuses on deriving estimating equations by simplifying the expression involving the summation of terms related to yi and parameters theta. It breaks down the equation into separate terms, emphasizing the goal of choosing parameters theta_0 and theta_1 to satisfy two estimating equations: one equating the difference between observed and estimated values to zero, and another involving the relationship between these values and their predictors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One over n times the summation of yi minus yi, i hat is equal to zero. And then the second equation is on the right hand side. What I'll do is I'll derive the first step. So deriving the first step means that what does it mean for this equation to be equal to zero? Is there any way that we can simplify it further? So what I'll do is I'll go ahead and try to... The compose all of these tree terms that are inside the parentheses and inside the summation into separate terms. Meaning that what I'll do is I'll say that this equation is equal to 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi. That's the first term. And then I'll stay minus 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of teta 0 hat. Right? And then minus... 1 over n times this dimension of i from 1 up to n of theta 1 hat x i equal to 0. children.title: Optimization of Theta Values in Estimation
children.summary: The segment discusses the optimization process for theta values (θ0 and θ1) in a function, where the partial derivatives are set to zero to find the optimal values, denoted as θ0 hat and θ1 hat. It emphasizes that these optimal values are derived from the conditions of the estimating equations. The segment also touches on simplifying expressions such as the average of y values in the context of these calculations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Can someone tell me why my tetas right now turned into tetahats? Because it's optimized to zero. Remember in the previous slide when I was on the previous slide here. Actually, yeah, right here when I was driving all of these values, the tetah 0 and tetah 1, they were just normal tetah 0 and tetah 1 of the function. I actually derived it and calculated the partial derivative with respect to tetah 0, with respect to tetah 1. At the point that you actually set it equal to 0, which is what we have right here, I set this equal to 0, then tetah 0 will become tetah 0 hat, because that's the optimum theta 0. And theta 1 will become theta 1 hat, because I'm also setting this one equal to 0. So that's at the point that you're calculating the optimum and you're finding the exact optimum values by setting the partial derivatives equal to 0, that's when theta 0 and theta 1 will turn into theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. Let me go back to this place that we were. So that's why I have theta 0 hat and theta one hat right here as I'm solving. And then at the end, we'll get to what is the actual value for theta zero hat and what is the actual value for theta one hat. So let me continue. What is the value? What is the simplified version of one over n times summation of yi values? That's why barn, that's average of why values. Right? The other thing, what is the simplified version of this? children.title: Estimating Equations and Simplifications
children.summary: The segment discusses the simplification of terms in estimating equations. It explains that summing a constant value, Teta 0 hat, n times results in n times Teta 0 hat, which simplifies to Teta 0 hat when multiplied by 1/n. The last term involves pulling out Teta 1 hat from the summation, leading to Teta 1 hat times the average of x_i, which is represented as x bar. The discussion emphasizes the simplification process and the relationships between the terms in the estimating equations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Teta 0 hat is a constant value. I'm summing it up n times, that this is going to be n times Teta 0 hat, and then I'm multiplying 1 over n. So this is equal to just teta zero hat. So the second term is teta zero hat. And then what is the last term? So the last term, there's no easy way to simplify the except for saying that I'm going to pull out theta one hat outside of the summation because it's a constant value. So then I'll have theta one hat times the summation of i from one up to n of x i's, which this one right here is also going to be x bar because that's the Sorry, there's one over n. One over n times the summation of x i's is going to be x bar. Again, setting it equal to zero, kind of cleaning up what I wrote here. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Deriving Estimators in Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The discussion focuses on deriving the estimator for theta zero hat in simple linear regression, emphasizing the relationship between the mean of y and the slope times the mean of x. It revisits the process of defining simple linear regression, selecting the mean squared error as the cost function, and calculating partial derivatives to find the optimal value for theta zero hat. The goal is to solve two estimating equations that relate the observed values to the estimated values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: y bar minus teta zero, hat minus. PEPTA 1 Times X bar is going to be equal to zero. Right? What does this tell you? This one is telling you that remember when I was calculating the y interest I'm always saying that teta zero hat is equal to y bar minus a slope times x bar. This is exactly that. This is deriving exactly the same equation for the teta zero hat that we had from before. just drive the true. The crazy sequence of defining the simple linear regression to going through the finding the cost function we chose mean a squared error as our cost function. We calculated partial derivatives, we set partial derivatives to equal to zero in order to find out what is the optimum tetaheus zero valiant that's a one value. children.title: Estimating Equations and Objective Function Definition
children.summary: The discussion covers the simplification of the first function leading to the derivation of theta 0 hat as y bar minus the slope times x bar, establishing a formal definition of the objective function. The aim is to select parameters 00 and 01 to resolve two estimating equations that include conditions for the residuals and their connections with the independent variable. Additionally, the process for finding theta 1 hat involves combining two estimating equations, specifically subtracting the first equation multiplied by x bar from the second. An algebraic method is used to derive theta 1 hat, focusing on calculations from equation 2, which incorporates x bar to address the x i term, factoring out common terms related to the differences between actual and predicted values.

children.title: Estimating Equations and Objective Function Definition
children.summary: The discussion focuses on simplifying the first function to derive that theta 0 hat equals y bar minus the slope times x bar. This leads to a formal definition of the objective function. The goal is to choose parameters 00 and 01 to solve two estimating equations, which include conditions for the residuals and their relationships with the independent variable.

children.title: Finding Theta 1 Hat Using Estimating Equations
children.summary: The focus is on determining the value of theta 1 hat by combining two estimating equations. The approach involves taking the second equation and subtracting the first equation multiplied by x bar from it. The goal is to solve for the parameters 00 and 01 using the equations provided, which include conditions for the residuals and their relationships with the independent variable.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The only thing that we need to now do is to figure out what is this theta 1 hat. But we still have two equations. We just looked at one of the equations. If we combine the two equations together, which I'm not going to try to derive it manually, but I'm going to walk you through the steps, is... It's going to help us to find out Teta 1 hat. The trick that we're going to use here is that we're going to take equation 2. We're going to subtract equation 1 times x bar from it. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Deriving Teta 1 Hat from Estimating Equations
children.summary: An algebraic approach is presented to derive Teta 1 hat, focusing on the calculations involving equation 2, which incorporates the average of x (x bar) to address the x i term in the equation. The process includes factoring out common terms, specifically the differences between actual and predicted values (y i and y i hat), to simplify the estimating equations. The goal is to choose parameters 00 and 01 to satisfy two estimating equations related to the model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is just an algebraic trick that you don't need to know and you should not be expected to know in the exams. But this is what I'm using in order to derive how we can find the Teta 1 hat. So if I go ahead and do the calculations, So what you're seeing is that this is equation 2 right here that we're seeing. This term right here is the equation 1 multiplied with x bar or the average of x. You can kind of guess why I multiplied it with x bar. This is because the equation 2 has that x i term dangling there and it just need to have something of the nature of x for to accompany it. And that's exactly what I'm using it. So what I can do right now is going to be I'm factoring out all of the y i's minus y i hats because that's the term that's in common between them. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Deriving Theta Estimates from Equations
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of the optimum estimate for y (y hat) using the equation y hat = theta zero + theta one times x. It explains the substitution of theta zero with the derived equation (y bar - theta one hat times x bar) and simplifies the equation to isolate theta one hat. The resulting equation highlights the relationship between the differences of actual and average values of y and x, demonstrating how theta one hat is influenced by the variance of x.

children.title: Deriving Theta Estimates from Equations
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of the optimum estimate for y (y hat) using the equation y hat = theta zero + theta one times x. It explains how to substitute theta zero with the derived equation (y bar - theta one hat times x bar) and simplifies the equation to isolate theta one hat. The resulting equation relates the differences between actual and average values of y and x, indicating how theta one hat is influenced by the variance of x.

children.title: Correlation, Regression Derivation, and Model Evaluation
children.summary: The segment covers the relationship between correlation and the derivation of simple linear regression, explaining that the regression equation's left-hand side can be expressed as the product of the correlation coefficient (r) and the standard deviations of x and y. It emphasizes defining a mathematical objective function for modeling in machine learning, leading to optimal coefficient values determined by the mean squared error cost function. Key points include that the average of residuals equals zero and their orthogonality to predictor variables in simple linear regression. Upcoming discussions will focus on the implications of residuals' orthogonality and the introduction of estimating equations. Model evaluation techniques are outlined, including data visualization, computing statistics, and assessing performance through mean squared error and root mean squared error. The importance of understanding errors in model fitting and visualizing residuals to ensure no patterns in residual plots is also highlighted.

children.title: Correlation and Simple Linear Regression Derivation
children.summary: The segment discusses the relationship between correlation and the derivation of simple linear regression. It explains that the left-hand side of the regression equation can be expressed as the product of the correlation coefficient (r) and the standard deviations of x and y. The speaker outlines the process of defining a mathematical objective function for modeling in machine learning, emphasizing that it is not based on guessing. The derivation leads to the determination of the optimal values for the coefficients (theta 0 and theta 1) using the cost function, specifically the mean squared error. Key points include that the average of residuals equals zero and the sum of residuals multiplied by x_i also equals zero.

leaf_chunk.audio: Um, so kind of a reminder from what we know from correlation, you know that the correlation was equal to this equation right here, which sounds similar to what we have in the left hand side. So what we can do is we can say that the left-hand side right now is going to be equal to r times a standard deviation of x times a standard deviation of y because the only thing that this this equation is missing compared to this equation is these denominators right here. So the left-hand side of this equation is r times a standard deviation of x times a standard deviation of y. The left-hand side is my theta one hat times the variance or a standard deviation in the square of x. And then what's happening here is that I only have one unknown. I can directly solve it. Now that the one hat is going to be equal to our times the standard division of x over a standard division of y. This kind of sounds like magic, but now we formally define how actual machine learning people do modeling. They don't guess. They basically have a mathematical objective function that they define. They define the family of functions or the model pattern, which is what we did, but define what is the equation for simple linear regression. But we said that, okay, depending on the tetasero and tetabone value, it's going to be a different function or a different line that we're mapping. How do we find out how good that function is with defined the cost function? We actually use minus squared error, we actually venture calculating partial derivatives in order to find out, Find out the optimum values for teta 0 and teta 1, which got us the teta 0 hat and teta 1 hat that we have. So this is where we are. What we got at the end was that was the two kind of final notes that I told you when I was doing the derivation. I said that the average of residuals is equal to zero or the summation of residuals is equal to zero. And I also said that the residual multiplied with the x i is equal to zero.

children.title: Orthogonality of Residuals in Modeling
children.summary: Next week, the discussion will focus on the orthogonality of residuals to predicted variables, explaining the implications of this relationship in modeling. It is emphasized that residuals must average to zero and be orthogonal to the predictor variable in simple linear regression (SLR) to ensure the model is appropriately fitted. Additionally, estimating equations are introduced, which help derive estimates and understand the model's focus.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I think on Tuesday of next week, we're going to talk about the fact that this means that the residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables. And we're going to see what that means. This is two vectors are orthogonal to each other and that's how the shape of the line is always is the terministically calculated once you have all of the data points that you have. We have two more minutes, but I just want to throw it out because you need for you need this for discussions that are happening today and that's about evaluating the model. children.title: Model Evaluation Techniques Overview
children.summary: Model evaluation involves several key steps: visualizing data and computing statistics, such as correlation coefficients. If the correlation is acceptable, linear regression analysis follows. Additionally, performance is assessed by calculating the mean squared error and its square root, known as the root mean squared error, to convert the error into the original data units.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Your TAs are going to talk about evaluating the model a little bit in discussions today, but effectively that's the last step of the data of the modeling that we're going to go through. There are three things that we're doing generally even we're going to evaluate how well the model is performing. performing. The first thing is that a lot of time we would go ahead and visualize the data and we will compute the statistics. Again, we will calculate the correlation coefficient if the correlation coefficient is acceptable then we do the linear regression analysis. The other way that we evaluate the performance of the model is that we will calculate mean a squared error and we will calculate the square root of that. That's called root minus the square root error. And the reason that we calculate the square root of it is because we want to turn the error into the original unit of the data. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: Your TAs are going to talk about evaluating the model a little bit in discussions today, but effectively that's the last step of the data of the modeling that we're going to go through. There are three things that we're doing generally even we're going to evaluate how well the model is performing. performing. The first thing is that a lot of time we would go ahead and visualize the data and we will compute the statistics. Again, we will calculate the correlation coefficient if the correlation coefficient is acceptable then we do the linear regression analysis. The other way that we evaluate the performance of the model is that we will calculate mean a squared error and we will calculate the square root of that. That's called root minus the square root error. And the reason that we calculate the square root of it is because we want to turn the error into the original unit of the data.

children.title: Evaluating Model Fit and Residuals Visualization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of understanding errors in model fitting, particularly when calculating square roots of squared errors to return to original data units. Visualizing residuals is crucial; a residual plot should not display any patterns, indicating that the linear regression model is a poor fit if patterns are present. The goal is to achieve a random and equal distribution of errors across the data. Upcoming discussions will cover constant models and additional loss functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because the error is in the second order. It's when we're calculating a square root of the errors, we were squaring the error. So the unit was in the squared units. So by taking a square root, we're going back to the original unit of the data. So that value is going to be meaningful if you're doing any estimation. The last thing that we will do, and this is what you will see in discussions, is we will visualize your residuals. So when we go ahead and visualize residuals, what we want to see is that if these are the four different data sets that I'm dealing with and I'm passing the regression line through them, you can kind of see that the regression line matches one or two of them perfectly, but it's not a suitable choice for the rest of them. If you go ahead and visualize the residual, meaning that on the y-axis, you plot the residuals and the x-axis, you plot x, we don't want this residual plot to have any kind of pattern. Which is what you're seeing in this first case that residual plot does not show any pattern, but you're seeing that residual plot absolutely shows the pattern, meaning that linear regression was not a good choice. Or residual plot, in this case also shows the pattern. also in this case shows the pattern, right? So what we want at the end is that once you build the residual plot, you really want to make sure that the errors are kind of randomly distributed and equally distributed across X and also across the residual kind of dimension. Your T's are going to more talk about this a little bit more in the discussion. Thank you for coming. We're going to talk about constant model and more loss functions on Thursday. leaf_chunk.audio: Because the error is in the second order. It's when we're calculating a square root of the errors, we were squaring the error. So the unit was in the squared units. So by taking a square root, we're going back to the original unit of the data. So that value is going to be meaningful if you're doing any estimation. The last thing that we will do, and this is what you will see in discussions, is we will visualize your residuals. So when we go ahead and visualize residuals, what we want to see is that if these are the four different data sets that I'm dealing with and I'm passing the regression line through them, you can kind of see that the regression line matches one or two of them perfectly, but it's not a suitable choice for the rest of them. If you go ahead and visualize the residual, meaning that on the y-axis, you plot the residuals and the x-axis, you plot x, we don't want this residual plot to have any kind of pattern. Which is what you're seeing in this first case that residual plot does not show any pattern, but you're seeing that residual plot absolutely shows the pattern, meaning that linear regression was not a good choice. Or residual plot, in this case also shows the pattern. also in this case shows the pattern, right? So what we want at the end is that once you build the residual plot, you really want to make sure that the errors are kind of randomly distributed and equally distributed across X and also across the residual kind of dimension. Your T's are going to more talk about this a little bit more in the discussion. Thank you for coming. We're going to talk about constant model and more loss functions on Thursday.
